title: Conditioning and feeding a leather sofa
subtitle: Clean, condition, and protect a leather sofa to restore suppleness and prevent cracking
excerpt: How to clean and condition a leather sofa using pH-neutral cleaner and a leather conditioner or beeswax cream, removing surface grime, restoring moisture to the hide, and buffing to a natural sheen.
sourceNotes: The Practical Upholsterer (1917). Cassell's Household Guide (leatherwork). W.H. Sutherland The Art of Leatherwork (1926).

glossaryTerms.definition: A product that replaces the natural oils in a dried leather hide, restoring flexibility and preventing cracking. Good conditioners contain lanolin, beeswax, or neatsfoot oil. Avoid silicone-based products: they seal the surface rather than feeding the leather and can cause delamination on top-coated hides.

Two main types of upholstery leather. Aniline leather has no protective coating and absorbs liquid readily: it develops a patina with use but marks easily. Protected (pigmented) leather has a polymer topcoat that resists stains but cannot absorb conditioner through the coating. Most modern sofas use protected leather; the label or supplier should confirm the type.

content.text: Leather dries out over time from sunlight, central heating, and normal use. The process begins long before cracking is visible: the first sign is a chalky or matt surface. A good
can restore suppleness before cracking starts. The cleaning and conditioning method differs for
: check the type before choosing products.

A pH-neutral leather cleaner (not washing-up liquid or household surface spray, which are too alkaline for leather). A leather conditioner appropriate to the leather type: beeswax cream for aniline leather, a water-based emulsion conditioner for protected leather. Several clean, soft lint-free cloths. A soft brush for the seams. Do not use shoe polish or floor wax on upholstery leather: they contain solvents that can lift the surface coating.
Step 1, vacuum and brush the seams

Remove loose cushions and vacuum the sofa surface with the brush attachment to remove dust and crumbs from the panel faces and seams. Use a soft brush to work along the piping and seam lines: dust packed into the seams accelerates cracking at the seam edge. Work over the whole sofa before applying any liquid product.

Apply a small amount of pH-neutral leather cleaner to a clean cloth and work it into the leather surface in circular motions, one panel at a time. The cloth should be damp, not wet: excess moisture on aniline leather can leave tide marks. Buff the panel with a dry cloth before moving on. Rub along the piping and seam lines with a slightly damp soft brush to remove ingrained grime. Allow the whole sofa to dry for 30 minutes before conditioning.

Apply a small amount of conditioner to a clean cloth and work it into the leather with a circular motion, covering one panel at a time. Apply a thin coat: too much product leaves a greasy film. Pay extra attention to the seat fronts and arm tops where the leather flexes most. On aniline leather, the conditioner is absorbed almost immediately. On protected leather, it sits on the surface longer: buff it lightly after 10 minutes.

After 20 minutes, buff the whole sofa with a clean dry cloth to remove any residue and raise a light sheen. The leather should feel soft but not sticky or greasy. If any area still looks matt or dry, apply a second thin coat of conditioner and buff after a further 20 minutes. A second coat is often needed on the arm tops of a sofa in a sunny room.

A beeswax cream applied after the conditioner on aniline leather gives extra water resistance without blocking the pores. Work the cream in with a soft cloth, allow 10 minutes, then buff. For a leather that has already started to crack, a leather repair compound can fill the cracks before conditioning. Conditioning will not reverse deep cracking, but it will stop it advancing further.

Condition a leather sofa twice a year: once in spring and once before the central heating goes on in autumn. Keep the sofa away from direct sunlight and radiators. A sofa in a sunny bay window or next to a radiator needs conditioning three to four times a year. Blot any spills immediately with a dry cloth without rubbing: rubbing spreads the liquid into the pores.

attrs.symptom: Conditioner leaves white tide marks on the leather after drying
attrs.cause: Too much product was applied and has dried before it was fully buffed off.
attrs.fix: Dampen a clean cloth and work over the tide mark with gentle circular motions to re-emulsify the conditioner residue. Buff dry. Apply thinner coats next time and buff before they dry on the surface.

attrs.cause: Too much conditioner was applied; the excess is sitting on the surface of protected leather.
attrs.fix: Buff vigorously with a dry cloth to work the excess conditioner into the leather or remove it. Wipe with a barely damp cloth if buffing alone does not resolve it. Use a much thinner coat next time.
